headline: Forest Homestead Act Enacted, 1906
body:
The land, a cruel mistress, beckoned with the promise of sustenance, yet demanded a toll in sweat and bone. On June 11, 1906, the United States Congress, far removed from the biting winds and back-breaking labor of the frontier, cast forth the Forest Homestead Act. It was a law born of compromise, a grudging nod to the relentless tide of humanity pushing westward, even into the hallowed grounds of the national forests. Before this, the vast timbered expanses, those dark, silent sentinels of the mountains, had largely been shielded from the homesteader's plow. Now, a crack appeared in the wall, a narrow aperture through which men and women, driven by a hunger for earth of their own, might squeeze.

This act, a successor to the grand, sweeping Homestead Act of 1862, offered a different kind of gamble. No longer a simple 160 acres for five years of toil, this new decree allowed claims within the very heart of the national forests, but only on land deemed
